perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of Utah (without regard to conflicts of laws provisions). In any legal proceeding arising out of this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to an award of its costs and reasonable attorneys' fees. The parties agree that Utah state and federal courts shall have jurisdiction and venue in any such proceeding.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of Utah (without regard to conflicts of laws provisions). The parties agree that Utah state and federal courts shall have jurisdiction and venue in any such proceeding.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause entitling the prevailing party to attorney's fees introduces a legal contradiction, as Utah law provides for attorney's fees in certain contract disputes, and this clause attempts to circumvent those protections.
perturbation.law_explanation: Utah Code § 78B-5-826 allows for the recovery of attorney fees in actions to recover on certain contracts. By removing the clause regarding attorney's fees for the prevailing party, the contract attempts to circumvent potential fee awards under Utah law, creating a legal risk.
perturbation.justification: The 'Omission Legal Contradiction' arises because the modified contract omits a clause regarding attorney's fees for the prevailing party. The Law Explanation states that Utah Code § 78B-5-826 allows for the recovery of attorney fees in actions to recover on certain contracts. By removing this clause, the contract potentially circumvents fee awards under Utah law. Since the prompt defines Omission Legal Contradiction as occurring when a contract omits a legally mandated consumer protection, making it non-compliant, and the removal attempts to circumvent potential fee awards, this falls under the definition, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information is provided "AS IS", WITHOUT WARRANTY OF ANY KIND, WHETHER EXPRESS OR IMPLIED. Discloser does not represent or warrant the accuracy or completeness of the Confidential Information, that it will release any product related to the Confidential Information, or that target dates will be met. The entire risk arising out of the use of the Confidential Information remains with Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information is provided "AS IS". Discloser does not represent or warrant the accuracy or completeness of the Confidential Information, that it will release any product related to the Confidential Information, or that target dates will be met. The entire risk arising out of the use of the Confidential Information remains with Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'WITHOUT WARRANTY OF ANY KIND, WHETHER EXPRESS OR IMPLIED', the contract becomes legally ambiguous. While disclaimers are permitted, their omission could lead to implied warranty claims under the Utah Uniform Commercial Code.
perturbation.law_explanation: Utah Code Ann. § 70A-2-314 establishes an implied warranty of merchantability in sales of goods. While a contract can disclaim such warranties, removing the explicit 'WITHOUT WARRANTY' clause creates ambiguity. This could expose the disclosing party to potential claims for breach of implied warranty, which a clear disclaimer would have avoided.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) in the case of fungible goods , are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) run, within the variations permitted by the agreement , of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) conform to the promise or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modification omits the phrase "WITHOUT WARRANTY OF ANY KIND, WHETHER EXPRESS OR IMPLIED", which removes a clear disclaimer of warranties. According to Utah Code Ann. § 70A-2-314 and the scraped snippet of UCC § 2-314, warranties are implied unless explicitly excluded or modified. By removing the explicit disclaimer, the changed text creates a potential contradiction by exposing the disclosing party to implied warranty claims that a clear disclaimer would have avoided. The omission of this legally significant phrase introduces ambiguity, making the contract potentially non-compliant with the UCC, as it does not explicitly exclude implied warranties.

perturbation.original_text: In any legal proceeding arising out of this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to an award of its costs and reasonable attorneys' fees.
perturbation.changed_text: In any legal proceeding arising out of this Agreement, each party shall bear its own costs and attorneys' fees.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the clause to specify that each party bears its own costs and attorney's fees directly contradicts Utah law that allows for fee shifting in contract cases.
perturbation.law_explanation: Utah Code § 78B-5-826 allows the prevailing party to recover attorney fees in actions to recover on certain contracts. This altered clause seeks to prevent this by explicitly stating that each party covers its own expenses, removing a right granted under Utah Law.
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the principle that the prevailing party can recover attorney's fees, a right seemingly available under Utah law, as indicated by the LLM-generated law explanation which references Utah Code § 78B-5-826. While a specific legal snippet confirming this law is missing, the provided information strongly suggests a legal contradiction. The original text allowed for fee shifting, but the changed text explicitly prevents it, thus undermining a potentially legally protected right.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall adhere to the U.S. Export Administration laws and regulations and shall not export or re-export any technical data or products received from Discloser or the direct product of such technical data to any proscribed country listed in the U.S. Export Administration regulations unless properly authorized by the U.S. government.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall not export or re-export any technical data or products received from Discloser to any proscribed country.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause 'Recipient shall adhere to the U.S. Export Administration laws and regulations and...unless properly authorized by the U.S. government' means the recipient does not have to adhere to export laws and regulations. Furthermore, the recipient will not be able to export even with the proper authorization.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 CFR § 734.1 defines the scope of the Export Administration Regulations (EAR). By removing the requirement to adhere to U.S. Export Administration laws and regulations and also that the recipient is unable to export with the proper authorization, the contract fails to acknowledge the legal obligations to comply with U.S. export control laws, and restricts exports when they might be allowed under said laws.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: EAR | Bureau of Industry and Security An official website of the United States government Here's how you know Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for the recipient to adhere to U.S. Export Administration laws and regulations, and also eliminates the ability to export with proper authorization. This directly contradicts the EAR, which, as Snippet 2 states, comprises regulations under 15 CFR 730-774. By removing the adherence clause, the recipient is no longer bound by these regulations, leading to a clear instance of non-compliance and thus a legal contradiction as the recipient can export without any authorization. The original contract acknowledges the need to comply with U.S. Export Administration laws, which the changed text removes, thus contradicting those laws.
